content_en:
As-Sarakhsi's treatment of zakat and fasting in Al-Mabsut provides the most detailed Hanafi analysis of these pillars available in classical literature, covering every major and many minor questions with full evidential grounding and comparative engagement with other schools.

On the question of when zakat becomes obligatory — specifically, whether the obligation arises at the beginning of the hawl year (when the nisab is first reached) or only at its end — as-Sarakhsi presents the nuanced Hanafi analysis. The obligation is considered to arise gradually throughout the year and crystallize at the year's end. This affects the question of partial ownership changes during the year — for example, if part of the zakatable wealth is gifted, sold, or destroyed before the year ends.

The Hanafi school's position on zakat of debt (dayn) receives elaborate treatment. Debts owed to a person are of three categories in Hanafi analysis: (1) dayn qawi (strong debt) — amounts owed for goods or services actually exchanged, which is fully zakatable when recovered after any number of years (paying all years' accumulated zakat upon recovery); (2) dayn da'if (weak debt) — amounts arising from non-trade contexts (like loans) which is zakatable only prospectively from the year of recovery; and (3) dayn bi-la asl (baseless debt) — amounts that arose without counter-value, on which no zakat is due. As-Sarakhsi defends this three-tier classification with detailed reasoning about the nature of ownership and the purpose of the hawl requirement.

For sawm, as-Sarakhsi analyzes the Hanafi position on the intention for Ramadan fasts. The Hanafi requirement that the niyyah can be made any time during the night or before midday of the fasting day (not necessarily the previous night) is defended with the hadith of 'A'ishah in which the Prophet formed the intention to fast while already in the morning. As-Sarakhsi distinguishes between the intention for Ramadan fasting (which must be specifically for Ramadan) and voluntary fasting (which may be initiated with general intention at any time).

The chapter on i'tikaf (mosque seclusion) presents the Hanafi position that i'tikaf is valid in any mosque where congregational prayer is performed — with the minimum being a mosque where the daily prayers are observed. The person in i'tikaf may not leave the mosque except for necessities (toilet, ghusl for junub) and must not engage in sexual relations. As-Sarakhsi addresses the question of a woman's i'tikaf: in the Hanafi school, women perform i'tikaf in their designated prayer space at home rather than at the mosque, reflecting the school's general principle of not requiring women to come to the mosque.

As-Sarakhsi closes the fasting chapter with extended discussion of the laylat al-qadr — the Night of Power in Ramadan. He presents the different scholarly opinions on which night is the Night of Power (the 27th being the most widely cited, based on the reports of Ubayy ibn Ka'b and others), and the recommendation to seek it in the last ten odd nights. The reward of worship on this night — 'better than a thousand months' (al-Quran 97:3) — makes the striving to find it among the most valuable acts of the Ramadan spiritual program.
